title: Building an Invoicing & Billing App in Laravel
subtitle: Invoices and line items, tax calculation, gapless numbering, statuses and payments, recurring billing, and PDF generation — the Laravel architecture behind an invoicing tool.

sections.h2: How an invoicing app fits together

blocks.p: An invoicing app is a money document that must total correctly, number sequentially, and track payment. The pressure: monetary math must be exact (never floats), invoice numbers must be gapless for compliance, an invoice is immutable once issued, and partial/late payments change its status.

blocks.diagram:
Customer
 └─ Invoice (number, status, currency, totals)
     ├─ Line Items (qty, unit_price, tax_rate, line_total)
     ├─ Payments (amount, method, date) ─► partial/full
     └─ PDF snapshot
 Recurring profile ─► generates Invoices on schedule

blocks.p: The defining action — issuing an invoice — assigns a number, freezes totals, and starts the dunning clock:

blocks.diagram:
Issue invoice ──► assign next gapless number (atomic)
   │ compute line totals + tax + grand total (integer cents)
   ▼ status: draft → sent ; due_at set
   ▼ render + store PDF snapshot ; email customer (queued)
   └─► scheduled job flips to overdue past due_at if unpaid

ul: Document model — invoice + line items, totals computed and frozen on issue.
Money & tax — integer minor units, exact tax rounding per line vs total.
Numbering & status — atomic gapless sequence; draft→sent→paid/overdue lifecycle.
Payments & recurring — partial payments, reconciliation, and scheduled generation.

blocks.callout: An invoice is an immutable, exactly-totalled, sequentially-numbered money document. The hard parts are precise money/tax math and gapless numbering under concurrency — not the form.
blocks.title: The mental model in one sentence

blocks.p: Floating point can't represent 0.10 exactly, so sums drift by cents. Store money as integer minor units (cents) or use a decimal/money library (e.g. brick/money). All arithmetic happens in integers; format to currency only at display.

An invoice hasMany line items (description, quantity, unit price, tax rate). Line total = qty × unit price; tax = line total × rate. The invoice subtotal, tax, and grand total are sums of line values, computed once and stored on the invoice at issue so the document is self-consistent forever.

Rounding tax per line then summing can differ by a cent from taxing the subtotal. Jurisdictions specify which is correct. Pick a documented rule (commonly round each line, then sum) and apply it consistently — this is a frequent source of off-by-a-cent disputes.

Many tax regimes require sequential, gapless invoice numbers. You cannot use the DB auto-increment id (gaps from deletes/rollbacks) or compute max()+1 (races). Use an atomic counter: a dedicated sequence row locked in a transaction (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE) so two concurrent issues can't claim the same or skip a number.

Once sent, an invoice must not change — you issue a credit note or a new invoice instead of editing. Lock line items and totals after issue; only status and payments evolve. This is both a legal and trust requirement.

draft → sent → (partially_paid) → paid, or sent → overdue past the due date if unpaid. A scheduled job flips unpaid sent invoices to overdue and can send reminders (dunning). Status is derived from payments + due date, recorded explicitly for querying.

A payment records amount/method/date; an invoice is paid when payments sum to the total (handle partials and overpayment). Recurring billing stores a profile (interval, template) and a scheduled job generates the next invoice — idempotently, so a double-run doesn't bill twice.

blocks.callout: Using floats for money or max(number)+1 for numbering are the two classic invoicing bugs — one drifts cents, the other duplicates/skips legally-required sequence. Use integer cents and an atomic counter.
blocks.title: Most common misconception

blocks.p: An invoicing app builds immutable money documents from line items, totals them in integer cents with a defined tax-rounding rule, assigns gapless numbers atomically, and tracks payments to drive status.

An issued invoice is a printed receipt: once it prints you can't scribble on it — a mistake means a refund slip (credit note), not an edit. The register counts in whole pennies, never fuzzy fractions. Receipts come off the roll in strict order with no missing numbers — you can't have receipt 1002 without 1001. And the till is balanced by matching payments to receipts.

blocks.diagram:
BEGIN
  SELECT value FROM sequences WHERE key='invoice' FOR UPDATE; -- locks row
  next = value + 1
  UPDATE sequences SET value = next WHERE key='invoice';
COMMIT  -- no other tx can read the row until commit → gapless

blocks.p: Each lesson is one idea. Code illustrative.

blocks.h3: Lesson 2 — Compute and freeze totals on issue
Lesson 4 — Lock the document after issue

items.q: Q1. Why must money never be stored as a float?
items.a: Floats can't represent decimals like 0.10 exactly, so sums drift by cents over many operations. Store integer minor units (cents) or use a money library; format to currency only at display.

items.q: Q2. Why can't you use the DB auto-increment id or max(number)+1 for invoice numbers?
items.a: Auto-increment leaves gaps (deletes/rollbacks), violating gapless-sequence tax rules; max()+1 races under concurrency, producing duplicates or skips. Use an atomic locked sequence row.

items.q: Q3. Describe the atomic gapless-numbering technique.
items.a: In a transaction, SELECT the sequence row FOR UPDATE (locking it), increment, and commit. Concurrent issuers block until the lock releases, so each gets a unique consecutive number with no gaps.

items.q: Q4. Why are totals computed once and stored on the invoice at issue?
items.a: So the document is self-consistent and immutable: later price/tax changes can't alter a sent invoice. The stored subtotal/tax/total are the legal record.

items.q: Q5. What's the per-line vs per-invoice tax-rounding issue?
items.a: Rounding tax on each line then summing can differ by a cent from taxing the subtotal once. Jurisdictions mandate one; pick and document a consistent rule to avoid off-by-a-cent disputes.

items.q: Q6. How should you correct an error on an issued invoice?
items.a: Don't edit it — issue a credit note or a corrective invoice. Issued invoices are immutable for legal and trust reasons; only status and payments may change.

items.q: Q7. How is recurring billing made safe against double runs?
items.a: Idempotent generation: firstOrCreate keyed on (profile_id, period) with a unique constraint, so a re-run of the scheduler can't create two invoices for the same period.

blocks.p: Re-do the quiz from memory; redraw the atomic-number-claim each session.

head: When
Session (~10–15 min)
Goal
rows: Read packet; quiz with answers open.
Quiz closed-book; restate integer-cents + gapless rules.
Flashcards; explain per-line vs per-invoice tax aloud.
Interleaved quiz; write the locked-sequence code from blank.
Teach gapless numbering to someone in 5 minutes.
Closed-book interleaved pass; misses restart Day 1.

blocks.callout: Retrieving just before you'd forget produces the strongest gain.
blocks.title: Why the gaps grow

blocks.p: Topics shuffled to train choosing the right idea.

items.q: M1. Customers report totals occasionally off by a cent. Two likely causes?
items.a: (1) Float money arithmetic drifting. (2) Inconsistent tax rounding (per-line vs per-invoice). Fix: integer cents everywhere and one documented rounding rule.

items.q: M2. An auditor finds invoice numbers 1001, 1003, 1004 — 1002 missing. How did it happen and how prevent?
items.a: A number was claimed then the transaction rolled back, or numbers come from auto-increment with a deleted row. Prevent by claiming the number only on successful issue via a locked sequence committed atomically with the invoice.

items.q: M3. Two staff issue invoices at the same instant and get the same number. Fix?
items.a: Replace max()+1 with SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on a sequence row inside a transaction so concurrent issuers serialize and each gets a unique consecutive number.

items.q: M4. A sent invoice was edited to change an amount. What should the system have done?
items.a: Blocked the edit (immutable once sent) and required a credit note / corrective invoice. Only drafts are editable.

items.q: M5. The recurring scheduler ran twice after a deploy and billed some customers twice. Root cause?
items.a: Non-idempotent generation. Use firstOrCreate keyed on (profile_id, period) with a unique index so re-runs are no-ops.

items.q: M6. When would you store money as a decimal column instead of integer cents?
items.a: When using a money library/DB DECIMAL with controlled scale and you need fractional minor units (e.g. unit prices at 4 dp for some currencies/commodities). Either is fine if arithmetic is exact and rounding is explicit — the cardinal rule is no binary floats.

ul: Build totals two ways — float vs integer cents — and watch the float version drift over many lines.
Break numbering on purpose — use max()+1 and fire concurrent issues to reproduce duplicates.
Write immutability + gapless tests first before the UI.
Add PDF generation — render an immutable PDF snapshot stored per invoice.
Stretch goals: multi-currency with FX snapshots, partial-payment allocation, dunning email sequences, credit notes, and Stripe/Paddle reconciliation via webhooks.

flashcards: The two hard parts of invoicing
Exact money/tax math and gapless invoice numbering under concurrency.
Why never floats for money
0.10 isn't exact in binary; sums drift by cents. Use integer minor units.
Why not auto-increment id for invoice numbers
Deletes/rollbacks leave gaps, violating gapless-sequence tax rules.
Why not max(number)+1
It races under concurrency → duplicate or skipped numbers.
Atomic gapless numbering
SELECT sequence row FOR UPDATE in a transaction, increment, commit — serializes issuers.
Why freeze totals on issue
Makes the invoice self-consistent and immutable; later price/tax changes can't alter it.
Per-line vs per-invoice tax
Rounding per line then summing can differ a cent from taxing the subtotal; pick one documented rule.
Correcting an issued invoice
Issue a credit note / corrective invoice — never edit; issued invoices are immutable.
Invoice status lifecycle
draft → sent → partially_paid → paid, or sent → overdue past due_at.
Deriving 'paid'
Sum payments; paid when ≥ total, partially_paid when >0, handle overpayment.
Idempotent recurring billing
firstOrCreate keyed on (profile_id, period) with a unique index prevents double billing.
Marking overdue
A scheduled job flips unpaid sent invoices past due_at to overdue (and can send dunning).
glossary: An immutable money document billing a customer.
A billed row: quantity × unit price, plus tax.
Integer cents — the safe representation of money.
Strictly sequential invoice numbers with no missing values.
A locked counter row used to claim numbers atomically.
A document reversing/correcting an issued invoice.
A payment less than the invoice total.
A template + schedule generating invoices periodically.
The documented method for rounding tax (per line/total).
An unpaid invoice past its due date.
Matching recorded payments to gateway/bank events.
